item.title: Re: Quick question on this one:
item.content:encoded: RAISA censors individual PoI names because from an in universe perspective, it would be quite easy to track down Dmitri when you've got all the information about them, as a Foundation mole. As I understand it, its simply to limit immediate interference from a hostile force or level 1 smarmy researchers.

item.title: Re: Missing?
item.content:encoded: Where is the typo in addendum 4? As for the wrong numbering, that was my fuckup and will be fixed shortly.

item.title: Missing?
item.content:encoded:
I found a typo in the collapsible part of addendum 4.

And one more thing……Where is Addendum 5?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The inclusion of Hiemal is interesting. It appears their presence serves as like having the other potential problems to "hibernate".

Though how a bunch of carnivores eating themselves keeps grass and herbivores from overpopulating I am having a bit of trouble following.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted because I really like that a bunch of carnivores went "You know what? [EXPLETIVE] those [EXPLETIVE] herbivores! We don't need them!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are they bothering to stop the ones in custody? It makes them happy and it's not apparently hurting anyone …

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I've edited the WikiWalk proposal to include your suggestions.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: 2952 is not at all involved in the canon, nor is 2709 or 2376. 2889 is definitively part of the canon as a Verdant entity anomaly. 2547 and 3807 are both Veldt entities and would be better choices for crosslinking here.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

In your author post, you confirmed that this is part of a "canon" of sorts involving several of your SCPs. For this reason, I propose linking SCPs such as SCP-2889, SCP-2376 and SCP-2952.

Adding this sentence to the end of the Special Containment Procedures:

Under no circumstances are any animals infected with SCP-3240 to be let within five meters of SCP-2952 or any associated SCP-2952-2 entities.

Editing this:

Affected animals show no other behavioral anomalies except that they no longer engage in hunting behaviors.

To this:

Affected animals show no other behavioral anomalies except that they no longer engage in hunting behaviors1.—

Adding this to the end of the description:

Due to their similarities, it is theorized that SCP-3240, SCP-2889 and SCP-2547 are related. It is of note that sites of SCP-2547 manifestations report high levels of infection of SCP-3240 after the manifestation, and that additional anomalous activity has ensued in the area of SCP-2889 after SCP-3240's neutralization.

Editing this:

Afflicted individuals have been located among the homeless population of several cities, most prominently in Moscow, Salt Lake City, and Marrakesh.

To this:

Afflicted individuals have been located among the homeless population of several cities, most prominently in Moscow, Salt Lake City, Egypt and Marrakesh.

Footnotes
1. In addition, affected animals are immune to SCP-2709.

item.title: Re: Quick question on this one:
item.content:encoded: He's not a skip - the phenomenon itself is the skip, so it's more like he's a PoI than anything else. This isn't an anomaly where it makes sense to make all of the affected individuals skips - there would be too many to count. And PoIs are usually involved in the creation of SCPs, so I decided just to refer to him by his name.

item.title: Quick question on this one:
item.content:encoded: I may have missed it someplace, but is there a reason for using the child's name instead of it's SCP designation? Just from my own standpoint, my brain kept trying to read the D. Arnatsiaq as Dr. Arnatsiaq, and so I kept looking around trying to figure out why the child was being called Doctor. Then my brain caught up with my eyes, and realized it was his initial. So used to seeing interviews with the skip name as their designation, even if the researchers weren't using their designation to help them stay calm. Overall, an incredibly good read, and after the explanation for Heimal, I really dig the idea of it being added to the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why did that one guy break off his rib if the carnivores need their bones?

item.title: Re: Fascinating
item.content:encoded: It's supposed to be a deliberate "fuck you" to demonstrate what happens when hunters stop doing what they're doing: the magical/mythological equivalent of a strike, in essence: "You want to say we're evil? Fine, see what happens when we don't do our jobs."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, late reply to a comment here, but it's relevant. Keter, Euclid, and Thaumiel have little to do with what they mean here on the wiki; I continued that tradition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was rereading this when I noticed something:

(All three goshawks begin to peel away the the skin and inner lining from his ribcage)

Don't think that extra the is supposed to be there.

item.title: Fascinating
item.content:encoded: I love the internal mythos that develops in this one - and I do mean develops. There's some great pacing here, with layer after layer being peeled away. I was able to see the conclusion coming eventually, but in a good way - the sense that I'd finally hit the revelation of the inevitable logic of it all. One little nitpick, though: Describing these autosarcophagic animals as "hunters" in the religious texts feels a little… weird. I mean, isn't hunting precisely what they've stopped doing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this quite a bit. The feeling of initial "oh, just a crazy cult with some weird disease" quickly transforms into "oh sh*t, they're right and this is real" and I love it. Very unsettling, especially the child. Not sure how I feel about the object class, but it's not enough to ruin it for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I can find about the meaning of "hiemal" is "wintry". Are you sure you don't mean the god Hiemdall(r)? Because with him possessing foreknowledge, he fits much better than "hiemal", since this skip is a very good example of a situation where foresight would've prevented the Foundation from unwittingly causing the end of the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May I get a link to "The Rise of Pan" you mentioned, as I am unable to find much information regarding it

item.title: Re: I'm a little confused
item.content:encoded: Ohh. Thanks!

item.title: Re: I'm a little confused
item.content:encoded: From my understanding as a fellow reader, Hiemal denotes a group of anomalies which interact with one another such that they effectively contain one another.

item.title: I'm a little confused
item.content:encoded: Not about the story, but about the classification itself. What is "Hiemal"? And to add to that, what are all the new classifications about? I mean I like the new choices, but where do they fit in with the old 4?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fascinating, and with some really excellent pacing. The anomaly and its implications are verrry slowly and painstakingly revealed. I approve strongly of this.

+1, bringing it up to +200 :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually quite beautiful in spite of the bloody imagery or perhaps precisely because of it. I love the excerpts from the Veldt's Books they actually remind me a bit of Bloodborne or Cassilda's song from The King in Yellow. If this ever becomes a mythos the likes of The Hanged King, I'll be pleasantly surprised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am still puzzled by the relationship of plants/herbivores and carnivorous animals in this article, but that is exactly the point of reading SCPs, I enjoy this feeling. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of the Chocolate Sailor episode of Billy and Mandy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Different people - they're brothers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yet another skip where the Foundation manages to make things worse. The fun thing about this one is that it's essentially a "damned if you do, damned if you don't" situation unless someone can come up with a third option that doesn't result in a bad outcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What an interesting take on the rise of/revenge of Pan! I was a little confused by one thing - are Vasily and Dmitry different people, or is this a typo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: skip-ception

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: come on kids, let's MAKE IT R E A L

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooo. Nice. I wonder if there is anything else on the mainlist that I can retroactively headcanon this Class to fit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hiemal basically means you've contained something that was successfully containing something else. That something else is now uncontained due to containment of the first skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to ask, what does it mean? Safe, Keter, Euclid, and Thaumiel all have specific qualifications, so what makes a Hiemal?

Edit: Reread the entry. So it's an anomaly that, when contained, its removal from its environment triggers the creation of one or more new anomalies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a new object class. Let's see if it catches on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been trying to figure out what the hell to do with this: it's clearly linked, but the two phenomena are hugely different, as will become apparent upon investigation.

When this is a mainlist, I may actually make a new object class for this - initial containment success caused generation of new anomalies, requiring new procedures.

In terms of locked box, it would be "you managed to stuff it in the box, but then five minutes later it caused a different thing to break out of ITS box."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really well done, and I enjoyed reading it. I especially appreciated here was the understanding of ecosystems and all the fun specifics that came with it. Nice details. Good dialogue. The narrative arc of the SCP builds well, and the payoff is satisfying.

One issue for me:

Dr. Hessen: I … Good god. Good god.

Dr. Hessen stands and turns, clasping a hand to her mouth. After a minute, she returns to the desk.

This isn't realistic or believable dialogue — it's a common cliche seen very often in writing and never in real life. Dr. Hessen is a smart person. She's already had these thoughts. And even if she hadn't, she'd probably just go silent shortly, as opposed to these dramatics.

Stood out like a sore thumb because the rest of the dialogue is very good.

Another minor issue: the anomaly at the end is clearly related to SCP-3000, meaning 3000 itself is not the anomaly, merely an effect of the anomaly (which is also causing the plant stuff at the end). It's also very much not Neutralized. There's no reason researchers would write it that way, so I'd recommend tweaking. (I can believe they might not have rewritten the article yet — but then they wouldn't have changed it to Neutralized, because everyone who ever looked at the article at any point of time knows it's not.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second question: no, it was not. What is that?

They are manifest cosmic forces of nature. The Red is the source of Animal Man's power and is the power of animal life. The Green is the source of Swamp Thing's power and is the power of plant life. The Rot is the power of decomposition (and maybe fungal life, not sure) that is trying to corrupt the other two.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First question: I was considering the plant growth and the behavior to be so clearly different that it would be best to treat them as different, if linked, SCP designations.

Second question: no, it was not. What is that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand why this is Neutralized. The plant growth at the end seems to be ongoing.

Was this inspired by DC Comics' The Red/The Green/The Rot mythology?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to say that I appreciate the fact that this only has one hidden block of text and doesn't go irritatingly in depth about ever single detail; the last point being very counter intuitive to horror. I didn't get much of a horror vibe from it but I could see where it could induce horror and this entry was amazingly well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3000 has been found to affect humans.

This is the part where I had to pause. I saw it coming from a mile away, and it still hit me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been kind of trying to juxtapose the spartan/austere ideal of those who ARE religious with the unbelievers, thus the insulting bags of blood comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm at a neutral vote. I think this starts out with a very strong premise. This thing introduces body horror and autosarcography (you learn a new word every day) in a way that's pretty fresh and interesting, especially with the whole "affects predators in harsh environments only" thing, which has a lot of cool associations with it.

I feel like execution of this premise could have been done better towards the end. I agree with some other people that the religious voice isn't really doing it for me, and I feel like that's the most prominent thing in the second half of the article that's trying to put on the creepy factor. That might be because I didn't really imagine something that you were associating with this spartan, barren force to have such lines as "you sordid, gluttonous bags of fat and blood," which strikes me as kind of indulgent. I also don't really get the pacing: it seems like if you want the world to end at the article's conclusion, you'd want to spend some more time examining that, but you rush through it fairly quickly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers
– hide block

I think you're in the minority WRT to the pacing - I'm going to have to figure out how the heck to make these religious folk more… present, I suppose.

Also - this is not the Veldt that's spreading. The Veldt has occupancy of the most desolate places, the Verdant from the most conducive to life. THAT'S why it spreads from where it does - it's where it's strongest.

Hey, thanks anyway. Much appreciated however you vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not meaning to keep harping on an article that's at +88, but:

+ show block
– hide block

I feel like moving the religious events before Dmitri's log kind of gets rid of the sledgehammer pacing that hit me the first time. His being the first human expression of this, and so unexpected, hit hard. That might not be coming back though. It's also screws up the pacing, in that it goes straight from talking about animals to mention "the religion associated with SCP-3000." Without the middle context of Dmitri's log, it's weird.

That might be unavoidable, because anything after that log is going to pale in comparison.

The new ending is…interesting. It no longer feels like a (still truly horrifying) cartoon villain organisation, which is good. It's still massively impacting major portions of the world, though, which is a little pretentious. I did really enjoy the implication that there is no normality: our precious existence has always existed in the balance between these two forces, and without thinking or knowing we've just seriously fucked it up. (Maybe if the veldt was rapidly spreading from where -3000 has been wiped out instead of just a massive band of Earth's surface.)

I'm almost talking myself into an upvote, but I'm just not quite there (the non-Dmitri human events just don't do anything for me), and I don't think I can read it too many more times without throwing up.

Edit: Don't get me wrong, some of the writing here is fucking fantastic and you have all my admiration. Just not my vote.

item.title: Well done
item.content:encoded:
Well done in general, I liked especially the rhythm in the religious texts. I love the refreshing casting of greenery as evil.

Couple of corrections:

Duplicate 'the' in Addendum 2:
"mass - the the footage is too low"

Possible 'identified'/'unidentified' swap in Addendum 2:
"An identified man next to the recorder of the footage vomits"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well it's certainly… gross.

I don't know. This feels like its treading too much ground that's been covered both by you and the author and on the site before. A lot of the religious ranting is kind of boring and sort of rambles to have too much of an impact. I'm growing a bit tired of the "string spooky phrases together to get horror" style on the site. I'll upvote because a lot of the imagery is striking and the idea of a predatory religion is interesting, but I just don't feel like this is bringing anything new to the table.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These accurately summarize my own feelings. Similar novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember the Verdant and Veldt concept came up in 19 a week or two back, and my curiosity was piqued. Now, you have my attention. I feel that more needs to be done with this concept. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't particularly scared by this, but it's a DAMN good article.

Also also we got some Shub Niggurath up in here. You think I didn't see that old goat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding all of this. I think my favorite part was probably the bit with the random in Salt Lake City. The rationalization of such a disturbing practice isn't the worst part; it's the fact that the writing makes you agree with him.

I'm reminded of Colonel Kurtz's ravings to Willard at the end of Apocalypse Now - you know that the guy's fucking nuts on some level, but it's madness propped up by a calculating sanity.

In conclusion: https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=-YIEJ4PIZKE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking fascinating. A religion based around autocannibalism to avoid ingesting plant material? Absolutely unique. And I love the implication at the end that they were actually right, and had an effect on the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ho-lee shit. That was disturbing as hell and I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In addendum 3, it says "areas in high animals" - Were in and high swapped?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All right, majorly changed this - the ending is entirely different, so if you read it before and didn't like it for that reason, I entreat you to take a look again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check it again - i removed that part, and added something else that I like much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While a lot of the imagery and descriptions are creepy, I feel like the vagueness of the cult's beliefs added to the fanaticism that made them unsettling. When the goat horns came out, my feelings of unease left. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only during SCP-3000 - otherwise they'd be dead as doornails.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting. Sapient nonhumans in fiction generally trend towards cooperation and self-sufficiency if they are herbivores, and towards domination of the weaker if they are predators.

Here all the imagery is completely reversed, predators are the ones being literally self-sufficient, which is aided by ectoentropic generation of matter and/or energy.

This affects predators (esp obligate carnivores), and also humans because of course it does. Does this affect omnivores such as rats?

Affected animals never show any form of pain response

Do they never ever show any form of pain response, or does this only concern the acts of cannibalism? If the former, then it's generally very very bad, unless the affected creatures regenerate any damage (not implied) or don't really run on biology per se any more.
